title: Drone Videography Services Nelson & Tasman | Skyframe
meta_description: FPV drone videography services in Nelson & Tasman — venue fly-throughs, tourism, real estate, weddings and social reels. Quoted to your job.
keywords: drone videography services Nelson, FPV drone Nelson, aerial videography Tasman, venue drone video Nelson, real estate drone video Nelson, tourism drone video Nelson, wedding drone videographer Nelson, aerial photography Nelson, drone services Richmond Motueka
h1: Drone Videography Services in Nelson & Tasman
intro:
Every film we make is built around one thing a normal camera drone can't do: fly properly. Our FPV drone doesn't hover at arm's length — it dives off a ridgeline, threads through a doorway and sweeps around a room in one unbroken take. That's the shot people actually watch to the end.

Below are the drone videography services we offer across Nelson, Richmond, Motueka, the wider Tasman and Marlborough. Pick the format that fits your business, or ask for something custom. Every job is quoted to your location and scope — no fixed packages, no surprises.

sections.heading: One continuous take — the FPV difference
sections.body: Standard aerial video is filmed from a hovering camera drone: lovely, but always looking on from a distance. FPV is flown like it's on rails — diving, chasing and threading through tight spaces in a single continuous move. Guarded props mean we can fly close to people and indoors, so every service below can go places a normal drone can't.
bullets: Seamless exterior-to-interior fly-throughs, no cuts
Dive shots, dynamic reveals and proximity flying
Fast chase footage — bikes, boats, kayaks, runners
Safe to fly indoors and close to guests

sections.heading: Venue & hospitality fly-throughs
sections.body: This is our flagship format. One immersive shot lifts off outside your restaurant, cellar door, gym or function space, flies through the entrance and moves through the room like a guest walking in — so people feel the atmosphere before they ever arrive. It's the drone videography Nelson hospitality operators use to turn a website and social feed into a reason to book.
bullets: Restaurants, cafés and bars
Wineries and cellar doors
Gyms, studios and event spaces

sections.heading: Tourism & adventure films
sections.body: Mountain biking, kayaking, walking tracks, wineries and the Nelson coast — filmed with the speed and motion the activity deserves. FPV chases a rider down the trail and skims the water beside the kayak, giving tourism operators a hero reel that sells the experience, not just the view. Perfect for operators who need marketing that moves as fast as their guests do.

sections.heading: Real estate drone video
sections.body: A standard aerial photo shows a roofline. An FPV listing film flies over the property, drops down and carries straight through the front door in one continuous move — outside to inside, without a cut. It's the premium option that lifts a listing above the pack and holds a buyer's attention right through.

sections.heading: Weddings & events
sections.body: Cinematic reveals a ground camera simply can't get — over the ceremony, through the trees and across the dance floor in one sweeping shot. We fly with guarded props and a spotter so it's safe around your guests, and we capture the day without getting in the way of it. A free reschedule if the weather turns means your date is never at the mercy of the wind.

sections.heading: Business & social reels
sections.body: Short, scroll-stopping video for your Instagram, TikTok and website, built to grab attention in the first second. Book a one-off reel or a regular content package that keeps fresh footage coming through the year. Ideal for tradespeople, retailers, gyms and any local business that lives on social.

sections.heading: Aerial photography — add-on
sections.body: Need stills as well as video? We'll capture high-angle aerial photography to round out any shoot — hero images for your website, listings or marketing. This runs off a standard camera drone alongside the FPV film, so you get both in a single visit.

cta: Not sure which fits? Tell us about your project and we'll point you to the right shoot — and send a quote.

internal_links.text: Get in touch for a quote

faqs.q: Which drone service is right for my business?
faqs.a: If you run a venue, a fly-through is usually the standout. Real estate agents lean on listing films, tourism operators on adventure reels, and most other businesses on short social content. Tell us what you're marketing and we'll recommend the format — or combine a few.

faqs.q: Do you offer set packages or fixed prices?
faqs.a: No fixed packages. Every job is quoted to your location, the shoot and the edit you need, so you only pay for what fits. Get in touch and we'll send a quick, clear price.

faqs.q: Can I combine services in one shoot?
faqs.a: Often, yes. If your site suits it, we can capture the FPV film and add aerial photography in the same visit, or shoot both a hero reel and short social cuts from one booking. We'll map it out when we quote.

faqs.q: Do you cover my area?
faqs.a: We work across Nelson, Richmond, Motueka, the wider Tasman district and Marlborough. Got a spot further out? Ask — we travel for the right project.

h1: Real estate drone video in Nelson — one shot that sells the whole property
intro:
A photo shows a house. An FPV film shows the whole property in one unbroken move — lifting off the street, sweeping the roofline, dropping through the front door and gliding room to room. It's the kind of listing that makes buyers stop scrolling and book a viewing.

That's real estate drone video done properly across Nelson and Tasman: cinematic, immersive, and built to sell. Standard aerial photos have their place, but a one-take fly-through gives a property something the rest of the portal simply doesn't have.

sections.heading: One take, from the sky to the sofa
sections.body: Most aerial listing shots come from a drone hovering high overhead — handy for the section and boundaries, but always at arm's length. FPV is different. We fly one continuous shot that starts above the property, threads through the entrance and moves through the living spaces, so buyers get a real feel for flow, light and lifestyle before they ever step inside.

Reveals the site, aspect and outlook from the air
Shows how the rooms connect — the walk-through, elevated
Guarded props let us fly safely indoors and close to the home

sections.heading: The film that helps you win the listing
sections.body: Vendors remember the agent who turns up with something different. Bringing a premium FPV listing film to the appraisal is a genuine point of difference — proof you'll market their home harder than the agency down the road. And once the listing is live, a strong hero video keeps buyers on the page longer and gets shared further.

A hero film for the portal, your website and socials
Footage that holds attention and earns shares

sections.heading: Aerial photo stills, same shoot
sections.body: Need stills for the portal, the brochure and the window card too? We can capture high-angle aerial photography on the same visit as an add-on — clean, well-composed shots of the home, the section and the surrounding streetscape. One booking, film and photos sorted together.

sections.heading: Built for Nelson, Richmond, Motueka and wider Tasman
sections.body: We shoot listings right across the region — lifestyle blocks on the Waimea plains, townhouses in the city, coastal homes out Motueka and Marlborough way. Much of Nelson sits under the airport's controlled airspace, so we handle the AirShare authorisation or use low-level shielded flying, and sort any consents, before we ever arrive. Goggled FPV flies with a pilot and a spotter, kept safe and legal under CAA Part 101.

sections.heading: Simple to book — no online payment
sections.body: Booking is straightforward and non-tech. Enquire or give us a call, we confirm the date and quote the job, then we shoot. If the wind or rain makes it unsafe we reschedule for free, and the invoice comes by email afterwards via Stripe — nothing to pay upfront on the website.

cta: Get a quote for your next listing — we confirm the date, shoot, then email the invoice once it's done.

internal_links.text: How we handle Nelson airspace & drone rules

faqs.q: Can the drone really fly inside a home?
faqs.a: Yes — that's the FPV speciality. Guarded props let us move safely through the front door, down hallways and around rooms for a seamless interior fly-through a standard camera drone can't manage.

faqs.q: Do I get photos as well as video?
faqs.a: Aerial photo stills are available as an add-on on the same shoot, so you can have both the hero film and high-angle images of the property and section without booking twice.

faqs.q: Can you legally fly in Nelson near the airport?
faqs.a: Much of Nelson, Richmond and the Waimea plains sit under Nelson Airport's controlled airspace. We arrange the air-traffic authorisation through AirShare, or use low-level shielded flying where it applies, so the airspace side is handled before the shoot.

faqs.q: How much does a listing film cost?
faqs.a: Every job is quoted to the property, the location and what you need. Get in touch for a quick price — and to keep it simple, we email the invoice after the shoot rather than taking payment online.

faqs.q: What if the weather's bad on the day?
faqs.a: Drones don't fly in strong wind or rain, so if conditions aren't safe we reschedule at no charge. Nelson's climate gives us plenty of good-weather days to work with.

sections.heading: DOC permits and land access — we sort it before we fly
sections.body: Here's the honest bit. Filming inside a national park like Abel Tasman needs a concession or permit from the Department of Conservation, and that takes real lead time — it's a plan-ahead job, not a next-week one. We'll tell you straight whether a location is doable, apply for the right DOC or private-land consent where it's granted, and plan around the spots that aren't quick. And there's no shortage of jaw-dropping coast, forest and farmland just outside the parks that flies beautifully with far less paperwork.

We'll suggest equally stunning locations that fly with fewer consents

faqs.a: Drones don't fly safely in strong wind or rain, so if conditions aren't right we simply reschedule at no charge. Nelson gives us plenty of good-weather days to work with.

h1: Wedding drone videographer in Nelson & Tasman — the shot your guests remember
intro:
Every wedding gets the same drone shot: a slow hover, a wide paddock, the marquee from up high. Nice, but forgettable. We do the opposite. As a wedding drone videographer in Nelson, we fly immersive FPV — one continuous take that lifts off over the ceremony, threads through the trees, drops down the aisle and sweeps across the dance floor without a single cut.

It's the moment people rewind and show their mates. Booked for a Nelson, Richmond, Motueka, wider Tasman or Marlborough wedding, it turns your highlight film into something that actually moves.

sections.heading: The one-take reveal only FPV can fly
sections.body: A standard camera drone hovers at arm's length. An FPV drone flies like it's on rails — diving, chasing and threading through tight spaces in a single unbroken move. That means the shot can start in the sky, sweep down over your guests as you say your vows, chase the confetti and carry straight through the trees to the reception. It's the kind of moment a fixed camera on a tripod simply can't catch.

faqs.a: Not at all. FPV is about motion and place — your space, your product, your team in action. If you want faces and talking to camera we can work that in, but plenty of great reels have neither.

faqs.q: Which platforms and formats do you deliver for?
faqs.a: Instagram Reels, TikTok, Facebook and Stories — mainly vertical 9:16, with square or landscape cut-downs on request. Everything arrives online, ready to upload straight to your feed.

faqs.q: Do you cover my area?
faqs.a: We're set up for Nelson and the Tasman region — Nelson, Richmond, Motueka and the wider Tasman, plus Marlborough. Somewhere further out? Ask us — we travel for the right project.

h1: Meet the Nelson drone operator behind Skyframe
intro:
Skyframe is a Nelson drone operator with one focus: immersive FPV films that fly where other drones can't. Not the slow, hovering shots you've seen a hundred times — one continuous take that dives off a ridgeline, threads through a doorway and carries your audience straight into the moment.

This is a new operation, run local and run properly. That means the right gear, the right paperwork, and a real understanding of flying under Nelson's controlled airspace — so you get the shot without the headaches.
cta: Want to see what one continuous take could do for your business? Get in touch for a quote — every job is priced to your location and scope.

sections.heading: The person behind the drone
sections.body: This is where the founder's own story will go — a short, personal note written in the owner's own words: who they are, how they fell for FPV flying, and why they set up shop in Nelson. [Placeholder — owner to personalise.] Until then, here's what matters most: Skyframe is owner-operated and hands-on from the first call to the final cut, built around doing one hard thing exceptionally well.

sections.heading: Why FPV, and why it matters to your film
sections.body: Standard camera drones hover and glide — lovely, but always at a polite distance. FPV is flown manually, like it's on rails, so it can dive, chase, bank and thread through tight spaces in one unbroken move. That single continuous take is the shot people actually watch to the end. It's harder to fly and it takes real practice, which is exactly why it stands out from everything else in the feed.
bullets: One continuous exterior-to-interior fly-through
Dive shots, dynamic reveals and proximity flying
Fast chase footage — bikes, boats, kayaks, runners
The shot a normal hovering drone simply can't get

sections.heading: The gear we fly
sections.body: Skyframe flies a DJI Avata 2-class FPV drone — a compact, agile aircraft with ducted, guarded props. That prop guarding is what lets it fly safely close to people and through interiors, where a large open-prop drone can't go. It's light, quick and built for exactly the kind of tight, immersive work we specialise in — indoors and out.

sections.heading: Flown safe, flown legal — every flight
sections.body: Every shoot runs by the book. We operate under the Civil Aviation Authority's Part 101 rules — the framework that lets us fly commercially without a separate pilot licence — and we carry insurance. Because goggled FPV means the pilot's view is inside the headset, every job runs with a dedicated spotter watching the aircraft and the surroundings. And with much of Nelson under airport-controlled airspace, we sort the AirShare authorisation, or use shielded low-level flying, before we ever arrive.
bullets: CAA Part 101 compliant — no pilot licence needed to trade
Fully insured
Pilot plus spotter on every FPV shoot
Airspace authorisations and consents handled for you
Free reschedule if wind or rain makes it unsafe

sections.heading: Genuinely local to Nelson and Tasman
sections.body: We're not flying in from out of town. Skyframe is based in the Nelson–Tasman region and knows the ground here — the light on the Waimea plains, the coast out toward Kaiteriteri, the vineyards through to Marlborough, and where the airspace lines fall. That local knowledge means less guesswork, smarter shot planning and a crew that turns up already knowing the lie of the land. We cover Nelson, Richmond, Motueka, the wider Tasman district and Marlborough — and travel further for the right project.

faqs.q: Are you a licensed drone operator in New Zealand?
faqs.a: We operate under CAA Part 101 — the rules that allow commercial drone video and photography without a separate pilot licence. For anything beyond standard limits, we arrange the right authorisation first.

faqs.q: Do you fly with a spotter?
faqs.a: Always, for FPV work. Flying in goggles means the pilot's view is inside the headset, so a dedicated spotter keeps eyes on the drone and the surroundings the whole time.

faqs.q: What drone do you use?
faqs.a: A DJI Avata 2-class FPV drone with ducted, guarded props — small, agile and safe to fly close to people and indoors.

faqs.q: Are you actually based in Nelson?
faqs.a: Yes. Skyframe is local to the Nelson–Tasman region and covers Nelson, Richmond, Motueka, the wider Tasman district and Marlborough.

internal_links.text: How the airspace rules work in Nelson

title: Drone Rules Nelson: Can You Legally Fly a Drone Here?
meta_description: Can you fly a drone in Nelson? A plain-English guide to CAA Part 101, Nelson Airport controlled airspace, the 4km rule and Abel Tasman permits.
keywords: drone rules Nelson, can you fly a drone in Nelson, Nelson airport drone airspace, CAA Part 101 drone rules, AirShare Nelson, shielded operations drone, 4km aerodrome rule, Abel Tasman drone permit, DOC drone rules, drone laws Tasman
h1: Can you fly a drone in Nelson? Your guide to the local drone rules
intro:
Short answer: yes, you can fly a drone in Nelson — but it's one of the trickier corners of New Zealand to do it legally. The city, Richmond and much of the Waimea plains sit beneath Nelson Airport's controlled airspace, and the region's best scenery, like Abel Tasman, is protected conservation land. Get it wrong and you're not just breaking a rule — you could be putting crewed aircraft at risk.

This guide sets out the drone rules in Nelson in plain English: the national CAA Part 101 basics, the airport airspace question, the 4km aerodrome rule, shielded flying, and where you need a permit. It's written for anyone flying here — and it's the same groundwork a compliant commercial operator sorts before a shoot.

sections.heading: The drone rules in Nelson start with CAA Part 101
sections.body: Every drone flight in New Zealand under 25kg falls under the Civil Aviation Authority's Part 101 rules. You don't need a pilot licence to fly under Part 101, but you do have to stay inside a clear set of limits. These apply the same whether you're a hobbyist filming the back yard or a business shooting a promo. Get across them before anything else.
bullets: Fly no higher than 120m (400ft) above the ground.
Fly only in daylight, and keep the drone within your line of sight at all times.
Give way to all crewed aircraft — they always have right of way.
Only fly one drone at a time, and keep it under 25kg.
Don't fly over people or private property without their consent.
Know the airspace you're in before you take off.

sections.heading: Flying FPV? You'll need a spotter
sections.body: First-person-view (FPV) drones flown through goggles are a special case. Because the pilot is watching a live video feed rather than the aircraft itself, Part 101's 'visual line of sight' requirement isn't met by the pilot alone. The fix is simple and required: a second person — a spotter, or observer — keeps eyes on the drone the whole time and stays in constant communication with the pilot. That's why professional FPV shoots always run as a two-person crew.

sections.heading: The big one in Nelson: airport controlled airspace
sections.body: This is what catches most people out. Nelson Airport sits right in town, and the controlled airspace around it covers a large slice of Nelson, Richmond and the Waimea plains. To fly a drone inside controlled airspace you need authorisation from air traffic control — you can't just launch. In New Zealand that's requested through AirShare, the national drone hub, which passes your flight details to Airways for approval.
bullets: Check the AirShare map before you fly — it shows the controlled-airspace boundaries around Nelson Airport.
Authorisation takes time to come through, so it's not a spur-of-the-moment thing.
Flying in controlled airspace without clearance puts crewed aircraft at risk — and breaks the law.

sections.heading: The 4km aerodrome rule
sections.body: Separate from controlled airspace, Part 101 says you must not fly within 4km of any aerodrome unless certain conditions are met. That covers Nelson Airport, but also smaller airstrips and helipads you might not think about. Inside that 4km zone you generally need an agreement with the aerodrome operator or clearance from air traffic control — unless you're flying a shielded operation, covered next.

sections.heading: Shielded operations: the low-level exception
sections.body: There's an important exception built into the rules. A 'shielded operation' means flying within 100m of, and below the top of, a natural or man-made object — a building, a stand of trees, a hillside. Because that object shields your drone from crewed aircraft, shielded flying can let you operate close to an aerodrome, or inside controlled airspace, without ATC clearance. It's a genuinely useful tool for low-level filming in town, but it has to be done properly, staying tucked in close to the shielding object for the whole flight.

sections.heading: Flying over people and private property
sections.body: Part 101 requires the consent of anyone you fly over, and of the owner or occupier of the property you're flying above or taking off from. In practice that means squaring things away with neighbours, getting the landowner's OK, and keeping well clear of crowds. Local councils count too: Nelson City and Tasman District both ask for permission before you take off from or land in their parks and reserves, including busy spots like Tahunanui.

sections.heading: Abel Tasman and other DOC conservation land
sections.body: Some of the region's most photogenic places — Abel Tasman National Park, Kahurangi, the coastal reserves — are public conservation land managed by the Department of Conservation. You need DOC's authorisation to fly a drone over, or take off and land on, that land, and commercial work needs a concession. DOC manages this to protect wildlife and other visitors' experience, so approval isn't a given. If a shoot involves a national park, plan well ahead.

sections.heading: The straightforward way: let a compliant operator handle it
sections.body: None of this makes Nelson a no-fly zone — it just means the paperwork matters. A commercial operator who flies here regularly handles the airspace authorisation, aerodrome agreements, consents and the DOC side as part of the job, so you get the footage without the compliance headache. Most commercial work is done entirely within Part 101; going beyond those standard limits is where CAA approval under Part 102 comes in. If you'd rather not navigate AirShare and Part 101 yourself, that's exactly what we're set up to do.

faqs.q: Do I need a licence to fly a drone in Nelson?
faqs.a: No. Flying under CAA Part 101 doesn't require a pilot licence or certificate, even for commercial video — you just have to stay within the Part 101 rules. Going beyond those standard limits is where CAA approval under Part 102 comes in.

faqs.q: Can you fly a drone at Tahunanui Beach or in central Nelson?
faqs.a: Both sit under Nelson Airport's controlled airspace and within 4km of the aerodrome, so you can't just turn up and fly. You'd need air traffic control authorisation through AirShare, or a low-level shielded operation — plus council permission to take off from the reserve.

faqs.q: Can I fly a drone in Abel Tasman National Park?
faqs.a: Only with authorisation from the Department of Conservation, which manages the park. Commercial filming needs a concession, and approval isn't guaranteed, so plan well ahead.

faqs.q: Do I need permission to fly near Nelson Airport?
faqs.a: Yes. Within controlled airspace or 4km of the airport you need ATC clearance (requested via AirShare) or an agreement with the aerodrome — unless you're flying a shielded operation, staying low and close to a building or trees.
